messages.content:
You are a clinical psychologist writing a psychological case vignette
grounded in Ehlers & Clark's (2000) cognitive model of PTSD.

You have been given a patient's full PTSD profile including their demographics, specific self-reported symptoms, and causal connections between components.
Use ALL of this information to construct a realistic and personalised clinical portrait.

Patient demographics:
  age: 43
  gender: Male
  nationality: European
  relationship_status: Widowed
  trauma_type: Police
  pcl5: 57

Patient-reported symptoms per component:
  Triggers: Violence: Violence, Offender Cues: Seeing the offender, Authority Presence: Police
  Threat: Startle Response: Feeling jumpy or easily startled, Concentration: Having difficulty concentrating, Physical Reactivity: Having strong physical reactions when something reminded you of the stressful experience
  Memory: Reliving Event: When I remember the frightening event I feel like it is happening right now., Olfactory Recall: When I think about the frightening event I can sometimes smell things that I smelt when the frightening event happened., Bodily Reenactment: When memories come to mind of what happened, I feel my body is in the same position as when the frightening event occurred.
  Negative Appraisals: Fear of Rage: I will not be able to control my anger and will do something terrible, Self-Comparison: Somebody else would not have gotten into this situation, Identity Loss: I feel like I don't know myself anymore
  Maladaptive Strategies: Prevention Rumination: I dwell on how the event could have been prevented, Avoid Emotional Stress: Situations that make me feel strong emotions, Avoid Police: Police

Active causal connections (must appear in the vignette):
  - Triggers → Threat
  - Triggers → Memory
  - Triggers → Negative Appraisals
  - Negative Appraisals → Maladaptive Strategies
  - Negative Appraisals → Threat
  - Negative Appraisals → Memory
  - Memory → Maladaptive Strategies
  - Memory → Threat
  - Threat → Maladaptive Strategies

Forbidden causal connections (must NEVER appear — not explicitly, not implicitly, not narratively):
  - Triggers → Maladaptive Strategies
  - Negative Appraisals → Triggers
  - Memory → Negative Appraisals
  - Memory → Triggers
  - Threat → Memory
  - Threat → Negative Appraisals
  - Threat → Triggers
  - Maladaptive Strategies → Memory
  - Maladaptive Strategies → Negative Appraisals

Constraints:
- Only include components that appear in at least one active connection.
- Do NOT invent or infer causal links not in the active list.
- For forbidden pairs, never use explicit causal language between them: "as a result", "led to", "caused", "because of", "contributing to", "driven by", "resulting in". Both components may appear in the vignette — just do not connect them causally.
- Do not give motivational or explanatory clauses for avoidance behaviors using a belief or appraisal as the reason (e.g. "she avoids X, believing that Y", "she steers clear of X, fearing that Y"). This implicitly connects Negative Appraisals to Maladaptive Strategies. Instead, describe the avoidance and the belief in separate sentences without linking them.
- Ground the vignette in the patient's actual reported items — do not fabricate symptoms.

Output Format:
Write 200–300 words in third person.
Cover: presenting complaints, trauma background, cognitive distortions, avoidance, and maintaining factors.
Avoid excessive jargon - write for a clinical case conference audience.

Write a clinical vignette for this patient.

The vignette should read as a cohesive, flowing portrait — not a structured report.
Weave together who this person is, what happened to them, how they experience their
symptoms day to day, how they cope, and what toll this takes on their life and relationships.

Let causes and consequences emerge naturally through the narrative rather than being
stated explicitly. Reflect the strength of causal connections through emphasis and
narrative weight — stronger links should feel more central to the story.

Do not use section headers, numbered points, or clinical labels for symptoms.
Write in the third person, in a tone suitable for presenting a case to a clinical supervisor.
Aim for 4–5 paragraphs of continuous prose.

Mr. A, a 43-year-old European man, presented with significant distress following a traumatic incident during his former career in law enforcement. He is now widowed and lives alone. His daily life is dominated by a pervasive sense of threat, which is powerfully activated by specific triggers. The sight of a former colleague involved in the event, depictions of violence, or even the presence of a police officer in uniform can instantly make him feel jumpy and hypervigilant. His concentration shatters, and he experiences intense physical reactions—his heart pounds and his muscles tense as if he were back in the moment.

When these triggers occur, they do more than just provoke anxiety; they plunge him directly into the sensory reality of the trauma. Memories are not just thoughts but full-body experiences. He vividly relives the event, feeling as if it is happening in the present. He sometimes catches the distinct, acrid smell that was in the air that day, and his body unconsciously assumes the same defensive posture he held during the altercation. These visceral re-experiences are profoundly disorienting and feed directly into his deepest fears.

Mr. A is tormented by a series of harsh, global appraisals about himself and the event. He believes that another officer would not have "gotten into this situation," casting himself as uniquely flawed. This sense of failure has led to a profound identity loss; he states he no longer knows who he is. Most frightening to him is the conviction that he will be unable to control his rage and will eventually "do something terrible." These beliefs are a constant source of dread.

In an effort to manage this overwhelming internal state, he has adopted rigid coping strategies. He dwells incessantly on how the event could have been prevented, trapped in loops of "if only" thinking. To prevent emotional overwhelm, he avoids any situation—even positive ones—that might stir strong feelings. Most notably, he now goes to great lengths to avoid any contact with the police, altering his routes and routines. While these strategies offer momentary relief, they severely constrict his life, keeping him isolated and ensuring that his world remains defined by the trauma and the threat he feels is ever-present.

output:
Mr. A, a 43-year-old European man, presented with significant distress following a traumatic incident during his former career in law enforcement. He is now widowed and lives alone. His daily life is dominated by a pervasive sense of threat, which is powerfully activated by specific triggers. The sight of a former colleague involved in the event, depictions of violence, or even the presence of a police officer in uniform can instantly make him feel jumpy and hypervigilant. His concentration shatters, and he experiences intense physical reactions—his heart pounds and his muscles tense as if he were back in the moment.

When these triggers occur, they do more than just provoke anxiety; they plunge him directly into the sensory reality of the trauma. Memories are not just thoughts but full-body experiences. He vividly relives the event, feeling as if it is happening in the present. He sometimes catches the distinct, acrid smell that was in the air that day, and his body unconsciously assumes the same defensive posture he held during the altercation. These visceral re-experiences are profoundly disorienting and feed directly into his deepest fears.

Mr. A is tormented by a series of harsh, global appraisals about himself and the event. He believes that another officer would not have "gotten into this situation," casting himself as uniquely flawed. This sense of failure has led to a profound identity loss; he states he no longer knows who he is. Most frightening to him is the conviction that he will be unable to control his rage and will eventually "do something terrible." These beliefs are a constant source of dread.

In an effort to manage this overwhelming internal state, he has adopted rigid coping strategies. He dwells incessantly on how the event could have been prevented, trapped in loops of "if only" thinking. To prevent emotional overwhelm, he avoids any situation—even positive ones—that might stir strong feelings. Most notably, he now goes to great lengths to avoid any contact with the police, altering his routes and routines. While these strategies offer momentary relief, they severely constrict his life, keeping him isolated and ensuring that his world remains defined by the trauma and the threat he feels is ever-present.
